steps.title: Identify the grammatical relationship
steps.content: The blank separates the infinitive “to select” from “which sites to add next to the growing registry,” which serves as its direct object.

steps.title: Apply the punctuation rule
steps.content:
No punctuation separates an infinitive verb from its direct object. Identify the structure on each side: "gathers to select" is the infinitive, and "which sites to add next to the growing registry" is its direct object noun clause — nothing should come between them.
  • Choice A — select;: "gathers to select; which sites to add" — a semicolon joins two independent clauses, but "which sites to add" is not an independent clause. ✗
  • Choice B — select:: "gathers to select: which sites to add" — a colon introduces a list or elaboration after a complete clause, but "gathers to select" is not a complete clause on its own. ✗
  • Choice C — select,: "gathers to select, which sites to add" — inserting a comma between an infinitive and its object is not allowed; it creates a false pause mid-phrase. ✗
  • Choice D — select: "gathers to select which sites to add" — no punctuation between the infinitive and its object noun clause; grammatically clean. ✓
That matches Choice D — select.
